After this change, buildRetryMember uses only next_visible_at, a local retryBatchSeq value, the per-batch index, and the payload. Because retryBatchSeq starts at zero for every new Consumer, two separate Consumer instances, such as after a restart, during a rolling deployment, or in an accidentally scaled dispatcher, can generate identical retry members for the same envelope and next_visible_at. A second ZADD of the identical member will update the existing sorted-set entry rather than add another one, silently losing one retry. The previous timestamp-based batch token was weaker for deterministic ordering but provided process-independent uniqueness in normal operation. This is a reliability/integrity issue for delayed retry delivery, not an authentication or data-exposure vulnerability.

title: Legacy heal rewrites valid two-minute alarm schedules
description: ResolvePersistedTargetMinutes normalizes the persisted targetMinutes and then heals whenever the result has exactly two entries matching [alarmAdvanceMinutes, 1] with alarmAdvanceMinutes > 3. That shape is not uniquely legacy: it is also a valid explicit custom schedule. Since the provider calls this helper for persisted settings, an operator-configured or previously persisted two-target schedule like NOTIFICATION_ADVANCE_MINUTES=5,1 can be rewritten to the runtime policy [5,3,1], adding an unintended notification at 3 minutes and violating the stated constraint that explicit custom schedules remain untouched.

title: Bundle verifier untars untrusted archives without path checks
description: The newly added verify-full-bundle.sh script accepts an arbitrary bundle path from the user and immediately unpacks it with `tar -xzf` into a temporary directory. GNU tar will honor entries containing absolute paths or `../` segments, so a malicious bundle could overwrite files outside the temp directory when a reviewer runs the script. Because this is intended for verifying externally supplied review bundles, the extraction step should reject unsafe paths (e.g., use `--strip-components`, `--warning=no-unknown-keyword`, `--no-overwrite-dir`, and/or pre-validate archive entries) or extract with a safer tool.

title: Graduation PATCH now defaults missing flag to false
description: Adding `#[serde(default)]` to `Member.is_graduated` is appropriate because the Go upstream model serializes `isGraduated` with `omitempty`, so active members may omit the field. However, the same default was also added to `SetGraduationRequest.is_graduated`. That request is consumed by the admin-dashboard PATCH handler, so an authenticated request with body `{}` is now valid and is forwarded upstream as `isGraduated: false`, clearing the member's graduated status instead of rejecting the malformed request. This is an API validation/data-integrity regression rather than an auth bypass: the route remains behind the admin session middleware, and I did not find a new unauthenticated exploit path in this commit.

title: Stale delivery claims may never expire
description: The pre-send claim gate uses `claimAt` both as the value stored in `authorized_at` and as the reference time for deciding whether an existing claim is stale. This commit replaced the previous `time.Now()` claim timestamp with `resolveDeliveryClaimTime`, which prefers `outbox.NextAttemptAt`, `row.CreatedAt`, or `outbox.CreatedAt`. Those timestamps are stable metadata from when the item was created or became eligible, not when the lease is acquired. If a dispatcher claims a community/shorts post and then crashes before releasing or finalizing the claim, later dispatcher attempts will compute the same old `claimAt` from the unchanged outbox row. The stale check compares `state.AuthorizedAt` to `claimAt - timeout`; because both are based on the same fixed timestamp, the existing claim may never be considered stale. The post's deliveries can then be retried indefinitely as `pre-send claim` failures until the database row is manually repaired. Claim leases should be based on the current wall-clock time at claim acquisition, while event/detection timestamps should be tracked separately.

title: Review bundle script can leak .env secrets in tarball
description: The commit adds scripts/review/export-source-bundle.sh to generate a source bundle for reviews. The tar command excludes build artifacts and logs but does not exclude .env or other secret files that are intentionally gitignored. If an operator runs the script on a workstation with a populated .env (or other secret files in the repo root), the resulting tarball will include those secrets and can disclose API keys and credentials when shared externally.

title: Embedded Managed Close panics on nil runtimes
description: Several runtime/container structs now embed lifecycle.Managed instead of defining their own Close method. The new Managed.Close method checks whether the *Managed receiver is nil, but when Close is called as a promoted method on a nil outer pointer, Go must dereference the outer struct to reach the embedded Managed field before Managed.Close runs. As a result, calls such as `var r *BotRuntime; r.Close()` now panic. This is a regression from the removed explicit Close methods, which checked the outer receiver for nil. The crash appears limited to defensive cleanup paths and tests rather than attacker-controlled request handling.

title: Unbounded scraper worker count can crash scheduler
description: SCRAPER_WORKER_COUNT is parsed from the environment and only checked for being positive. The value is then propagated into the scraper scheduler and stored as workerCount. When the scheduler starts, it uses workerCount*2 as the buffered channel size and launches workerCount goroutines. A very large valid integer can overflow the channel capacity calculation and panic, or can exhaust memory/goroutines and prevent the scraper service from staying up. This appears to be an operational crash risk rather than a remotely exploitable security issue, because the input is deployment/operator-controlled.

title: Legacy indie org alias is no longer accepted
description: Changing `HolodexAPIParams.OrgIndie` from `Indie` to `Independents` updates all resolver comparisons that depend on that constant. `resolveStreamOrg` only matches `strings.ToLower(constants.HolodexAPIParams.OrgIndie)`, which is now `independents`; it does not retain `indie` as a backwards-compatible alias. As a result, callers using the existing documented API value `org=indie` for `/api/holo/streams/live` or `/api/holo/streams/upcoming` will receive `ErrInvalidStreamOrg` and a 400 response. This is a functional/API compatibility regression, not a security vulnerability.

title: YouTube scraper image healthcheck uses wrong default port
description: The newly added youtube-scraper image does not set SERVER_PORT=30005, but its Dockerfile declares and probes port 30005. The youtube-scraper binary loads the shared config, and the HTTP server binds to cfg.Server.Port. In the shared config, SERVER_PORT defaults to 30001. Therefore, running the new image without an explicit SERVER_PORT=30005 override starts the service on :30001 while Docker probes http://127.0.0.1:30005/health, causing the container to be marked unhealthy. In deployments that restart unhealthy containers, this can create a restart loop and deny service for the new scraper runtime. This is not an authentication or data-compromise issue, but it is an introduced availability bug.

title: Optional YouTube API key is now required at startup
description: YOUTUBE_ENABLE_QUOTA_BUILDING defaults to false, and the YouTube provider explicitly treats a missing API key as a valid state by returning a stats-only stack. This commit added isPlaceholderAPIKey() to Config.Validate() and classified the empty string as a placeholder. Because Validate() runs unconditionally during config.Load(), deployments that intentionally omit YOUTUBE_API_KEY while leaving quota building disabled now fail validation before the optional-provider fallback can run. Services such as hololive-bot and stream-ingester exit with code 1 on config.Load() errors, so this is an availability/configuration regression.

title: Bot .env example breaks local start script
description: The runtime configuration accepts both HOLODEX_API_KEY and the legacy HOLODEX_API_KEY_1, so the service itself can run with the new variable. However, the repository's local helper script still validates the copied .env file by requiring HOLODEX_API_KEY_1 specifically. After this commit, a user following the script's own instruction to copy hololive/hololive-kakao-bot-go/.env.example to .env will have HOLODEX_API_KEY but not HOLODEX_API_KEY_1, causing scripts/start-bot.sh to exit with "Required variable missing: HOLODEX_API_KEY_1". This is a deployment/availability bug, not a security vulnerability.

title: Fresh compose deploy can crash-loop on log directory permissions
description: The new compose settings set LOG_DIR to /app/logs/<service> and bind-mount ./logs to /app/logs for all four Go services. These images run as the non-root appuser uid 1000. In a fresh checkout, logs/ is not tracked or created by the deployment script; Docker bind mounts typically create a missing host source directory as root:root with mode 0755. The logger then calls os.MkdirAll(LOG_DIR, 0755) inside the container. Because appuser cannot write to the root-owned bind-mount parent, logger initialization returns an error and main exits. This can make hololive-bot, dispatcher-go, stream-ingester, and llm-scheduler fail to boot or crash-loop after a fresh deployment/recreate. A fix should pre-create and chown ./logs to uid/gid 1000 in build/deploy scripts, use per-service named volumes with correct ownership, or otherwise ensure /app/logs is writable by appuser before enabling LOG_DIR.

title: LLM HTTP/2 disable path can break TLS requests
description: The previous OpenAI client built a fresh http.Transport and disabled HTTP/2 by setting TLSNextProto to an empty map. The new code calls httputil.NewProfiledClient with DisableHTTP2=true. That helper clones http.DefaultTransport; in Go, Clone initializes/copies default HTTP/2 TLS settings, including TLSClientConfig.NextProtos containing h2. The helper then only replaces TLSNextProto with an empty map. This can leave the client advertising h2 in ALPN while not installing an HTTP/2 handler. If the LLM endpoint selects h2, net/http falls back to writing an HTTP/1.1 request on a connection the server expects to be HTTP/2, typically resulting in EOF/protocol failure. This is an availability/compatibility regression for LLM summarization against modern endpoints that support HTTP/2, not an authentication bypass or data compromise.

title: Parallel dispatch can reorder room notifications
description: FetchAndLock claims pending outbox rows ordered by created_at, and the previous dispatcher processed that slice sequentially. The new default MaxConcurrent value is 4, so the normal path now starts several SendMessage calls concurrently. If two or more pending notifications target the same room, a slower earlier send can complete after a later send, causing user-visible notification reordering. This is a delivery correctness/content-integrity regression, not an authentication, data exposure, or privilege-boundary vulnerability.

title: Nil-safe app helpers now panic on unset dependencies
description: Several helpers in the app package were refactored from nil-safe implementations into direct field or interface dereferences. A botSettingsApplier can still be constructed with a nil base SettingsApplier, but ApplyScraperProxy, ApplyAlarmAdvanceMinutes, and ScraperProxyRuntimeState now call through it directly. Container getters now dereference c.botDeps without checking whether dependencies were initialized. BotRuntime.Run also dereferences the receiver and Logger directly. In normal production construction paths, BuildRuntime requires a non-nil logger and the admin settings path builds a non-nil local SettingsApplier, so I did not find a remotely exploitable security vulnerability. However, this is an introduced crash regression for uninitialized or partially constructed runtimes and removes previously tested graceful behavior.

title: Fallback fail-fast can reject valid empty upcoming results
description: For upcoming streams, the scraper explicitly treats an empty event list as a normal successful outcome and does not fall back to the API for that channel. However, the new fail-fast helper is called with len(allStreams) as the primary success signal. If at least one channel is successfully scraped but has no upcoming streams, while another channel fails scraping and the API fallback for that failed channel also fails or is quota-blocked, len(allStreams) remains 0 and the function now returns an error. This is a false total-failure condition: not all primary targets failed; some channels were successfully checked and legitimately had no results. The condition should use primary success/failure counts, such as scraped == 0, rather than the number of produced stream items.

title: Nil context can panic milestone message formatting
description: In the previous implementation, formatChangeMessageWithContext converted a nil context to context.Background() before passing it to context.WithoutCancel. The current implementation calls context.WithoutCancel(ctx) directly. In Go, context.WithoutCancel panics when its parent context is nil. Normal production paths appear to pass non-nil contexts, and the public wrapper formatChangeMessage uses context.Background(), so this is not an externally exploitable security issue based on the inspected call chain. However, it is an introduced crash regression for direct internal/test callers that pass nil with a non-nil formatter and a milestone-crossing change.

title: Stale delivery failures can overwrite successful sends
description: FetchAndLock uses a timeout-based lock. If processing exceeds lockTimeout, another worker can claim the same PENDING delivery row and successfully mark it SENT. The MarkFailed path then updates the row using only WHERE id = ?, without checking that the row is still PENDING or still owned by the same lock holder. A stale worker can therefore overwrite a successful send with PENDING/FAILED, causing false failure state, duplicate retries, and possible duplicate notifications. This is a reliability/availability bug in the newly added repository; no current call sites for DeliveryRepository were found in this checkout, so it appears latent until this repository is wired into a dispatcher.

title: Nil guard removal creates low-impact reliability regressions
description: The changed code does not bypass authentication, expose secrets, weaken CORS/CSRF/session checks, or grant additional access. The affected paths are defensive nil cases: CacheError.Error now dereferences the receiver immediately, StreamIngesterRuntime.Run now dereferences the receiver during startup, and formatNumber/formatNumberKR now fall through to fmt.Sprintf for nil values. In the inspected application paths, StreamIngesterRuntime is constructed non-nil before Run is called, CacheError instances are produced by constructors returning non-nil pointers, and normal live-stream formatting converts nil viewer counts to 0 before rendering. Therefore this appears to be a low-impact reliability regression rather than an exploitable security issue.
